metrics.narrative: Virgin Australia articulated qualitative procurement pain on the 06 November 2025 discovery call but provided no quantified impact. Rahul Dev stated the objective was a market-sounding exercise to assess functional capability addressing VA's pain points, Oracle ERP integration complexity, user experience, aviation industry experience, implementation timeline, and high-level commercials to build a business case for budget approval. He noted they are running procurement manually with no current S2C system (Oracle S2C licensed via ServiceNow but never implemented). The vendor pack he promised would capture current-state pain points, use cases, and system landscape, but it was never delivered. No quantified cost savings, headcount reduction, cycle-time improvement, or error-rate target was stated. The business case he referenced is still unbuilt, so no ROI model exists. The gap: no quantified value case to justify the $500k investment and secure CFO or C-level budget approval.

champion.narrative: Rahul Dev (ICT Category Manager, Evaluator contact role) was the initial champion. On the 19 Nov 2025 demo call he stated confidentially that Zycus is a preferred option and that the Agentic AI and Use Case demo were highly regarded, signaling internal advocacy. He drove the agenda: requested the NDA, promised the vendor pack, scheduled the demo, and engaged his team. However, he departed Virgin Australia on 18 June 2026 (per Next Step entry dated 22 June), orphaning the deal. The intended handoff to Carly Fabian (Head of Procurement Operations & Governance, Decision Maker role) failed because she went on leave until 13 July 2026. The backup contact the relevant stakeholder (mentioned in the 22 June Next Step entry) is not in Contact Roles, not on any Avoma call, and their role, influence, and willingness to champion are unknown. The rep reached out to the relevant stakeholder on 22 June and is awaiting reply (7 days, no response as of 29 June). No other stakeholder—Bronwyn Sloan, Blair Krieger, Adelaide Callaghan, Michele Slater, Amanda Phillips, Matt Ely, Ponsak Rojjanasiraprapa—has engaged on a call or logged activity. The deal currently has no active champion, no internal sponsor, and no access to power. Champion strength: none (lost and not replaced).

competition.narrative: The competitive field per Salesforce Competitors__c is GEP Worldwide, iValua, Oracle, and SAP Ariba. Rahul Dev stated on the 06 Nov 2025 call that Virgin had shortlisted a few vendors for the market-sounding exercise and Zycus was one of them, confirming a multi-vendor evaluation. He noted they licensed Oracle S2C via ServiceNow but never implemented it, suggesting Oracle is the unused incumbent. On the call Carl Kimball (Zycus) positioned against Oracle explicitly, stating Zycus is a pure procurement platform with inherent advantages over an ERP vendor's S2C functionality, and noted Oracle/SAP are too expensive. No other competitor was named on either Avoma call. The Next Step entry from 19 Nov 2025 states Zycus is Rahul's confidentially preferred option, suggesting Zycus was leading at that point versus the other three. However, with no RFP released, no commercials presented, and the deal dormant for 7 months, the current competitive standing is unknown. The wedge: unified ground-up platform with patented AI (Merlin intake, agentic AI) and aviation reference (Delta Airlines 3+ year customer, renewed subscription) versus Oracle (unused incumbent, integration complexity) and GEP/iValua/Ariba (full S2P competitors). The risk: 7-month silence means any of the live competitors could have advanced while Zycus went dark.

identify_pain.narrative: Rahul Dev confirmed on the 06 Nov 2025 call that Virgin Australia is running procurement manually with no functional S2C system. He stated they licensed Oracle S2C via ServiceNow but never implemented it. The current-state pain points he intended to capture in the vendor pack (never delivered) were: (1) supplier management (onboarding workflow, data collection, qualification assessment, system integration), (2) sourcing (RFx, collaboration, vendor choice guidelines), and (3) contract management (repository, clause risk, lifecycle). He described the initiative as addressing VA's pain points around manual procurement processes, lack of workflow automation, and inability to enforce vendor choice or manage supplier risk. The pain owner is the procurement organization led by Bronwyn Sloan (General Manager Procurement) and Blair Krieger (Head of Strategic Procurement), though neither has engaged directly. Rahul was the operational pain owner (ICT Category Manager) driving the evaluation until his departure. The pain is confirmed and operational, but not quantified and not escalated to executive sponsorship.

decision_process.narrative: Rahul Dev described the decision process on the 06 Nov 2025 call as a phased market-sounding exercise (not a formal RFP at that stage): (1) initial vendor calls to understand capability, (2) 90-minute demo sessions, (3) vendor shortlisting based on demo confidence and lessons learned, (4) define next steps and approach post-shortlist, (5) build a business case for budget approval (potentially early 2026), and (6) formal RFP release. He stated the RFP was imminent end Feb 2026 (per 10 Feb Next Step entry), then revised to June 2026 (per 16 Mar Next Step entry). Neither deadline was met and no RFP has been released as of 29 June 2026. The call sequence shows: 06 Nov intro call with Rahul Dev (ICT Category Manager), 19 Nov demo call with Rahul and his team (attendees not named in transcript excerpt), then silence. No procurement leadership (Bronwyn Sloan, Blair Krieger), no C-level, no CFO, no EB joined either call. The approval chain (who must sign off, how many approvals, procurement vs finance vs exec) is unknown. Rahul's departure on 18 June and Carly Fabian's leave have stalled the process entirely.

sources: Avoma discovery call 06 Nov 2025, Rahul Dev (six criteria stated verbatim)
SF Next_Step__c 19 Nov 2025 (Zycus preferred, AI/use-case demo highly regarded)
decision_criteria.narrative: Rahul Dev outlined six evaluation criteria on the 06 Nov 2025 discovery call: (1) functional capability addressing VA's pain points, (2) Oracle ERP integration approach and complexity, (3) user experience and adoption potential, (4) aviation industry experience, (5) implementation timeline and effort, and (6) high-level commercials. He stated the demo session and vendor pack would allow Virgin to assess how each product aligns and build confidence to shortlist and define next steps. The 19 Nov 2025 demo call Next Step entry notes 'Rahul has indicated (confidentially) Zycus is a preferred option and Agentic AI and our Use Case demo were highly regarded,' suggesting Zycus was scoring well on criteria 1, 3, and possibly 4. However, the detailed vendor pack he promised—capturing current-state pain points, use cases, system landscape, and 90-minute demo agenda with questions—was never delivered, so the full decision criteria and weighting remain partially known. No formal scorecard, RFP rubric, or use-case validation process has been shared.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Zycus AI positioning (Merlin intake via Teams, agentic AI for autonomous supplier onboarding and sourcing workflows) scored highly on the 19 Nov 2025 demo call. Rahul Dev stated Agentic AI and the Use Case demo were highly regarded and positioned Zycus as his confidentially preferred option. Carl Kimball led with the unified platform, patented AI, and intake management differentiation on the discovery call. However, no AI commercials, ROI quantification, or use-case scoping has been presented, and the deal went dark before the AI story could be converted to budget approval. Under-positioned: AI appetite is confirmed but the business case and executive-level AI narrative (CFO/EB value story) were never delivered.

customer_expectations_fit.summary: Virgin Australia's six evaluation criteria from the 06 Nov 2025 discovery call align well with Zycus strengths, and the 19 Nov 2025 demo scored highly on functional capability, AI/use-case demonstration, and aviation experience (Delta reference). However, the vendor pack with detailed pain points, use cases, and system landscape was never delivered, so full fit validation remains incomplete. No commercials, pricing, or implementation timeline have been presented, leaving criteria 5 and 6 unaddressed. The Oracle ERP integration criterion (complexity assessment) has not been scoped. Overall fit is strong on capability and AI, partial on integration and timeline, and unknown on commercials.

value.how_we_win: Oracle is the unused incumbent (license owned from ServiceNow implementation that never went live). Virgin Australia has already rejected Oracle by non-implementation, signaling dissatisfaction with Oracle's S2C UX, complexity, or fit. Position Zycus as the procurement-pure-play alternative that delivers what Oracle could not: intuitive UX, rapid adoption, and innovation. Emphasize that owning an unused Oracle license is a sunk cost, and continuing with Oracle means repeating the same failed implementation.
